examples.title: Bresenham's Line Algorithm - Basic
examples.description: Standard implementation for lines with slope between 0 and 1
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BresenhamLine(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
 // Ensure line goes from left to right
 IF x1 > x2 THEN
 SWAP(x1, x2)
 SWAP(y1, y2)
 ENDIF
 
 dx = x2 - x1
 dy = y2 - y1
 
 // Initial decision parameter
 p = 2 * dy - dx
 
 x = x1
 y = y1
 
 PLOT(x, y)
 
 WHILE x < x2 DO
 x = x + 1
 IF p < 0 THEN
 // Choose lower pixel
 p = p + 2 * dy
 ELSE
 // Choose upper pixel
 y = y + 1
 p = p + 2 * dy - 2 * dx
 ENDIF
 PLOT(x, y)
 ENDWHILE
END
examples.explanation: This is the basic Bresenham algorithm for lines with slope between 0 and 1. The decision parameter p determines which pixel to choose at each step. If p < 0, the line passes below the midpoint so we choose the lower pixel; otherwise we choose the upper pixel and move diagonally.

examples.title: Bresenham's Line Algorithm - General
examples.description: Complete implementation handling all octants and slopes
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BresenhamLineGeneral(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
 dx = ABS(x2 - x1)
 dy = ABS(y2 - y1)
 
 // Determine octant and set step directions
 sx = 1 IF x2 >= x1 ELSE -1
 sy = 1 IF y2 >= y1 ELSE -1
 
 // Initialize decision parameter
 IF dx > dy THEN
 // Shallow line (|slope| < 1)
 err = dx / 2
 WHILE x != x2 DO
 PLOT(x, y)
 err = err - dy
 IF err < 0 THEN
 y = y + sy
 err = err + dx
 ENDIF
 x = x + sx
 ENDWHILE
 ELSE
 // Steep line (|slope| > 1)
 err = dy / 2
 WHILE y != y2 DO
 PLOT(x, y)
 err = err - dx
 IF err < 0 THEN
 x = x + sx
 err = err + dy
 ENDIF
 y = y + sy
 ENDWHILE
 ENDIF
 PLOT(x2, y2)
END
examples.explanation: This generalized version handles all line orientations by checking if |dx| > |dy| (shallow line) or |dy| > |dx| (steep line). For steep lines, it swaps the roles of x and y, iterating along the y-axis instead.

examples.title: Bresenham's Algorithm in C
examples.description: Practical C implementation for graphics applications
examples.explanation: This C implementation uses the error variable approach which is equivalent to the decision parameter. The error represents the accumulated difference. At each step, we check if moving horizontally or vertically reduces the error. This version handles all slopes and directions.
